disclaimer: This feed is an operational, country-code-oriented GeoIP artifact derived from public sanctions sources. It is provided for informational purposes only, is not legal advice, and is not a substitute for sanctions screening, export-control review, or advice from qualified counsel. Country-level blocking is necessarily coarse: many sanctions programs are targeted at specific people, entities, sectors, or regions rather than an entire country. OFAC explicitly states that it does not maintain a single list of countries with which U.S. persons cannot do business. Use this feed carefully and verify obligations against the underlying authorities.

ofac_broad_country.description: Country is part of the OFAC broad-country baseline.

ofac_present.description: Country has an OFAC country/program signal.

multi_authority_2_plus.description: Country is present in at least two authorities.

multi_authority_3_plus.description: Country is present in at least three authorities.

un_present.description: Country is present in a UN sanctions committee program.

curation.ai_summary: Conservative recommendation: block only four country-level codes where the provided evidence explicitly indicates a broad, country‑level baseline from a major sanctioning authority (OFAC) and/or comprehensive multilateral (UN) regimes. Other jurisdictions in the dataset show targeted or multi-authority sanctions regimes that do not, from the supplied evidence alone, justify automatic country-wide GeoIP blocking; these should be left out of the coarse blocklist or escalated for manual review.

ai_country_reviews.reason: EU/OFAC/UK sanctions present, but no explicit evidence in the provided sources of a broad, country‑wide blocking baseline to justify coarse GeoIP blocking.
Only UK regulation listed; evidence indicates targeted sanctions rather than an explicit country‑wide blocking baseline.

Multiple authorities (EU, OFAC, UK) maintain Belarus sanctions, but the provided sources do not state an OFAC ‘broad-country baseline’ or clear comprehensive country‑wide blocking justification in the evidence set.

EU/OFAC/UK/UN sanctions exist (UN 1533 Committee). UN involvement and multiple authorities suggest possible stronger controls; needs human review to assess whether measures are country‑wide or targeted.

EU/OFAC/UK/UN sources (UN 2745 Committee) are present. The evidence suggests significant multilateral measures but does not, by itself, prove a defensible blanket GeoIP block without human assessment.
OFAC entry explicitly notes a ‘broad-country baseline’ for Cuba in the provided evidence — a defensible basis for conservative country-level GeoIP blocking in an operational control set.
Only OFAC Ethiopia-related sanctions listed without explicit broad-country baseline evidence in the provided sources; treat as targeted unless further evidence is supplied.
EU and UK sanctions reference present, but no explicit indication in the supplied evidence of a country‑wide blocking baseline.
Only EU regime referenced; no evidence provided of a country‑level blocking baseline to justify GeoIP blocking.
UK and UN (2048 Committee) sanctions are present. UN listing suggests additional concern; recommend human review to determine whether blocking the whole country is defensible operationally.
OFAC Hong Kong-related sanctions are listed, but the evidence provided does not show an OFAC broad-country baseline for Hong Kong; treat as targeted unless further evidence is provided.
EU/UK/UN (2653 Committee) sanctions are present. Multilateral involvement warrants human review to determine whether measures justify a country-level block.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (1518 Committee) sanctions present. Given multi-authority and UN committee involvement, human review recommended before applying coarse GeoIP blocking.

Multiple authorities (EU, OFAC, UK, UN) are listed and OFAC is explicitly flagged with a ‘broad-country baseline’ in the provided evidence — supports a defensible country-level block for conservative operational control.

Comprehensive multilateral and national sanctions are present (EU, OFAC, UK, UN) and OFAC is explicitly noted as having a ‘broad-country baseline’ for North Korea in the provided sources — strong justification for country-level blocking.
OFAC Lebanon-related sanctions listed but no explicit broad-country baseline in provided evidence; treat as targeted.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (1970 Committee) sanctions are present. Multilateral measures suggest possible broader controls; human review recommended before coarse blocking.
Only EU sanctions regime referenced; no evidence in the provided sources of a country‑wide blocking baseline.
EU/OFAC/UK sanctions listed. Presence of multiple authorities suggests elevated concern, but evidence does not explicitly indicate a broad-country blocking baseline — human review recommended.
EU/OFAC/UK sanctions referenced. Given multiple authorities and known targeted measures, recommend human review to decide if country-level blocking is warranted operationally.
EU/OFAC/UK sanctions listed for Nicaragua. Multiple authorities are involved; human review recommended to determine whether blocking the whole country is appropriate.

EU (multiple RUS regimes), OFAC and UK sanctions are present. While significant, the provided evidence does not explicitly state an OFAC ‘broad-country baseline’ in these entries; given the complexity and operational impact, human review is recommended before applying an across-the-board GeoIP block.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (1591 Committee) sanctions are present. UN involvement and multiple authorities suggest stronger controls; human assessment needed to determine if country-level blocking is defensible.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (2713 Committee) sanctions present. Multilateral sanctions indicate elevated concern; human review advised before coarse GeoIP blocking.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (2206 Committee) sanctions are listed. Presence of UN committee and multiple authorities warrants human review to assess whether country-wide blocking is justified.
OFAC entry explicitly notes a ‘broad-country baseline’ for Syria in the supplied evidence, and EU/UK sanctions are also listed — supports a defensible conservative country-level block.
Only EU regime referenced (TUN); no evidence supplied of a country‑wide blocking baseline.
Only an EU regime is listed in the supplied evidence. No explicit broad-country blocking baseline in the provided sources to justify wholesale GeoIP blocking.

EU/OFAC/UK sanctions listed. Given multiple authorities but absence of an explicit OFAC broad-country baseline in the provided evidence, human review recommended before coarse blocking the whole country.
EU/OFAC/UK/UN (2140 Committee) sanctions present. Multilateral sanctions suggest significant controls; human review needed to judge whether a country-level GeoIP block is appropriate.
